Based on the gathered evidence, we see strong corroboration confirming that excessive use of adrenocorticosteroids indeed leads to decreased bone mineral density, which contributes to osteoporosis. Multiple references emphasize that these medications interfere with bone remodeling processes, particularly inhibiting osteoblastogenesis (the creation of new bone matrix-producing cells) and accelerating osteoclastic-mediated bone resorption—the opposite of desired physiological activity. Furthermore, some studies indicate how even minor disruptions caused by relatively lower-than-excessive dosage schedules could contribute indirectly via altered electrolyte balances affecting calcium utilization efficiencies, although the primary mechanism remains direct inhibition of bone-forming activities.

Based on the gathered evidence, we see strong corroboration confirming that excessive use of adrenocorticosteroids indeed leads to decreased bone mineral density, which contributes to osteoporosis. Multiple references emphasize that these medications interfere with bone remodeling processes, particularly inhibiting osteoblastogenesis (the creation of new bone matrix-producing cells) and accelerating osteoclastic-mediated bone resorption—the opposite of desired physiological activity. Furthermore, some studies indicate how even minor disruptions caused by relatively lower-than-excessive dosage schedules could contribute indirectly via altered electrolyte balances affecting calcium utilization efficiencies, although the primary mechanism remains direct inhibition of bone-forming activities.
